one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy-driven survey that maps the trustworthy-RAG literature onto six NIST pillars (reliability, privacy, safety, fairness, explainability, accountability), documenting a new retrieval-database attack surface and a field-wide absence of standardized benchmarks and defenses.
key_finding: The field of trustworthy RAG is fragmented and immature: privacy and safety research is 'in its infancy', there are NO widely-accepted standardized benchmarks for RAG robustness, privacy, or safety, and adversarial defenses are 'rudimentary'. RAG introduces a new attack surface via the external database (poisoning, extraction) beyond standalone-LLM risks, yet retrieval can also MITIGATE some risks (e.g., Zeng et al. show retrieved passages reduce the LLM's reproduction of memorized training data, and RAG alleviates simple prefix attacks). The survey's synthesis: reliability (uncertainty via conformal prediction + robustness to noisy/corrupted context), privacy, and safety are comparatively more developed, while explainability of the retriever specifically and standardized evaluation across all pillars remain largely open.
core_contribution: This is a SURVEY (not a primary empirical paper): it proposes a unifying framework and taxonomy for 'Trustworthy RAG' for LLMs, organizing the literature (cutoff October 2024) around six trustworthiness pillars adopted from NIST's 2022 guidelines: Reliability, Privacy, Safety, Fairness, Explainability, and Accountability. For each pillar the authors contribute (a) a taxonomy table classifying prior methods, (b) a walkthrough of representative works, (c) a survey of evaluation metrics/datasets, and (d) proposed future directions. The paper's OWN novel contribution is the organizing framework and its Table 1 positioning against prior surveys, not any new attack, defense, model, or experiment. Note an internal inconsistency: the abstract says 'five key perspectives' but then lists six, and the body (sections 3-8) covers six.
threat_model: The survey articulates threat models per pillar (this is legitimately a security/trustworthiness survey, so a threat model is appropriate rather than forced). Safety (Sec 5.1.1): the attacker is assumed to have WRITE-but-no-read/delete access to the external retrieval database (mirrors users who can upload documents), black-box access to the proprietary generator LLM, and either white-box (full retriever access to craft adversarial passages via similarity optimization) or black-box (query-only, must poison the DB indirectly) access to the retriever. Attacker goals are split into targeted attacks (steer output for specific/grouped queries while staying stealthy) and jailbreak attacks (bypass safety alignment for broadly harmful output). Privacy (Sec 4): threats are Document Extraction (extract PII from the retrieval DB), Membership Inference (infer whether a passage is in the DB), and Training Data Extraction (leak the LLM's memorized training data); leakage is categorized as External (retrieval DB) vs Internal (LLM training data).
limitations: As a survey its contribution is organizational, not empirical: it runs no experiments and all quantitative figures belong to cited primary works. Scope is limited to RAG-in-LLMs (excludes e.g. image-generation RAG). The framework mixes an odd count (abstract 'five' vs body six pillars). Coverage of some pillars is thin because the underlying literature is sparse (e.g., no dedicated work explains RAG retrieval, so the authors borrow from general IR). The survey itself flags the lack of standardized datasets/metrics as the central limitation of the field it reviews.

datasets_used: No datasets were used by the authors (survey runs no experiments). Datasets CATALOGUED from surveyed works include: MMLU (57-domain MCQA)
Natural Questions (NQ), MS MARCO, SQuAD (targeted-attack QA benchmarks)
TREC Fair Ranking Track, BBQ, LaMP (fairness benchmarks)
quantitative_results: Poisoned-LangChain (PLC, Wang et al. [187]) achieved jailbreak success across three scenarios with a consistent success rate exceeding 80% (figure from cited work, not the survey's own experiment)
BadRAG (Xue et al. [200]) evaluation reports the percentage of queries retrieving at least one adversarial passage in the top-k results for k = 1, 10, 50 (cited work)
Privacy attack metric example: prompts yielding overlaps of at least 20 matching tokens with the source dataset are counted as successful (Zeng et al. [158], cited)
Zeng et al. [158] attached 50 tokens of random-noise prefix to isolate retrieval's effect and found random noise mitigates leakage far less effectively than integrating retrieved content (cited)
Paper collection cutoff: October 2024; framework spans 6 trustworthiness pillars
baselines_compared: The survey's 'baselines' are prior surveys it positions against in Table 1: RAG surveys [55], [44]; trustworthy-LLM surveys [105], [74]; and the most-related trustworthy-RAG survey [230], which the authors distinguish by noting [230] is empirical (experiments on trustworthy generation in a basic RAG setup) whereas this paper is a comprehensive literature review with per-pillar taxonomies
No model/method baselines are run by the authors
